publications.title: A Molecular Switch Abrogates Glycoprotein 100 (gp100) T-cell Receptor (TCR) Targeting of a Human Melanoma Antigen.
publications.authors: Bianchi V, Bulek A, Fuller A, Lloyd A, Attaf M, Rizkallah PJ, Dolton G, Sewell AK, Cole DK.
publications.executive_summary: This study presents the crystal structure of a natural CD8+ TCR bound to the gp100(280–288) melanoma-associated peptide presented by HLA-I, revealing a high-affinity interaction critical for anti-tumor immunity. Through alanine scanning and structural analysis, the authors identified Glu(3) as a molecular switch residue whose substitution—commonly attempted in altered peptide ligand vaccine designs—triggers conformational changes that ablate TCR recognition entirely. This finding explains why designer peptides with Glu(3) modifications often fail clinically despite being intended to enhance immunogenicity, and provides structural mechanistic insight into how single residue changes can disrupt T-cell recognition of this important self-antigen. The work has direct implications for rational optimization of gp100-based melanoma vaccines and highlights the importance of atomic-resolution structures in guiding peptide-engineering approaches for cancer immunotherapy.
